1: A type: the anointing of Saul is the first royal anointing in Israel’s history and the inauguration of the messianic institution. The Hebrew root māšaḥ (to anoint) gives both the Hebrew māšîaḥ (Messiah) and, through the LXX translation christos (anointed one), the title Christ. Every royal anointing in the OT is a step in the chain that terminates at Christ’s anointing — not with oil on the head, but with the Spirit at his baptism (John 1:32-33: “the Spirit came down from heaven as a dove and remained on him... the one on whom you see the Spirit come down and remain is the one who will baptize with the Holy Spirit”). Samuel anointing Saul is the prototype; the Father anointing the Son with the Spirit is the fulfillment. Acts 10:38: “God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power.”
2: The first sign given to Saul — the two men at Rachel’s tomb — connects to Rachel as a site of mourning and death that anticipates new life. Rachel’s tomb (Genesis 35:19-20) marked the place of a death in childbirth; it becomes in Jeremiah 31:15 the site of “Rachel weeping for her children.” Matthew 2:17-18 quotes Jeremiah 31:15 for the massacre of the innocents at Bethlehem. The first sign to the first anointed king begins at a tomb — a shadow of the resurrection pattern: the first and final sign of the true Messiah begins and ends at a tomb. The good news comes from the place of mourning: the donkeys are found; death gives way to life.
3: The three men going up to God at Bethel — pilgrims carrying worship offerings — encounter the newly anointed king on the road. The anointed king meets worshippers going to God before his public installation. The theme of the anointed one crossing paths with those seeking God runs through the gospel accounts: Christ encounters seekers on roads (the Emmaus disciples, Luke 24:15), at wells (the Samaritan woman, John 4:7), and in the temple (the blind man after healing, John 9:35). Bethel (“house of God”) was one of Israel’s oldest worship sites, associated with Jacob’s ladder (Genesis 28:12-19); the encounter on the way to God foreshadows Christ as the way to God (John 14:6).
4: The two loaves of bread given to Saul by the Bethel pilgrims are a small sign of material provision on the journey. The theme of bread provided on the road of messianic calling runs through Scripture: manna in the wilderness, Elijah fed by the angel before his long journey (1 Kings 19:6-7), and the disciples given food by Christ on Emmaus road. Christ himself is the bread of life (John 6:35); the provision of bread to the anointed one on his commissioning journey is a shadow of the one who both receives and becomes the sustaining bread.
5: The prophesying company at Gibeah-elohim (“hill of God”) — playing instruments and prophesying with the Spirit — is an early example of the community of the Spirit that will characterize the messianic age. Joel 2:28-29 (“I will pour out my Spirit on all people; your sons and daughters will prophesy”) is the eschatological expansion of this pattern, and Acts 2 is its fulfillment. The Spirit’s communal manifestation here — not limited to one prophet but moving through a group — prefigures Pentecost, where the Spirit descends on the assembled community rather than on one individual alone.
6: A shadow: “the Spirit of the LORD will come powerfully upon you, and you will be changed into a different person” (wənehpaktā ləʾîš ʾaḥēr — literally “you will be turned into another man”). The Spirit’s transforming work on the anointed king anticipates the new-creation transformation promised in the new covenant (2 Corinthians 5:17: “if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come; the old has gone, the new is here”). Saul’s transformation is temporary and partial — the Spirit will later depart from him (1 Samuel 16:14). Christ’s Spirit-anointing is permanent (John 1:32: the Spirit “remained” on him), and the transformation he works in believers is eschatologically complete (Philippians 1:6: “he who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion”).
7: “God is with you” (hāʾelōhîm ʿimmāk) — the Immanuel formula. The assurance of divine presence given to the newly anointed king is the foundational covenant promise: “I will be with you” (Exodus 3:12; Joshua 1:9). The divine presence with the anointed one is the source of his effectiveness. Christ is Immanu-El — “God with us” — in his very person (Matthew 1:23, citing Isaiah 7:14), and his final commissioning word is “I am with you always, to the very end of the age” (Matthew 28:20). What was a conditional promise to Saul (“act as the situation demands — for God is with you”) becomes in Christ an unconditional and permanent presence.
8: The command to wait at Gilgal for Samuel — seven days, until he arrives with sacrifice — is the covenantal test of Saul’s obedience that he will fail in 1 Samuel 13. The waiting that leads to acting in God’s time rather than one’s own is a persistent theme: Abraham waiting 25 years for Isaac, Israel waiting 40 years in the wilderness, the disciples waiting ten days for Pentecost (Acts 1:4: “do not leave Jerusalem, but wait for the gift my Father promised”). Christ himself waited: 30 years of hidden preparation before his public ministry; 40 days in the wilderness before beginning to preach. The obedient waiting for God’s appointed time is the antithesis of Saul’s impatient sacrifice.
9: A shadow: “God gave him a new heart” (wayyahalek-lô ʾelōhîm lēḇ ʾaḥēr) is the language of covenantal inner transformation. The “new heart” given to Saul at his anointing anticipates the new-covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 (“I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts”) and Ezekiel 36:26 (“I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you”). Saul’s new heart does not sustain — he reverts to fear and disobedience. The permanent new heart that Jeremiah and Ezekiel promise is what Christ accomplishes through the Spirit’s indwelling (Romans 5:5: “God has poured out his love into our hearts by the Holy Spirit”). The signs all come true — confirming that YHWH’s word is reliable even when the recipient ultimately fails.
10: The Spirit of God coming powerfully upon Saul and his prophesying — placing him in the same Spirit-experience as the prophetic band — is the visible confirmation of anointing. The Spirit’s communal manifestation that crosses social boundaries (king with prophets) is the prototype of Pentecost’s boundary-crossing: Jews and Gentiles, men and women, old and young, all receiving the Spirit together (Acts 2:17-18 citing Joel 2:28-29). The Spirit’s falling on the newly anointed Saul among the prophets is the first scene of what will be a widening river of Spirit-outpouring that reaches its flood at Pentecost.
11: “Is Saul also among the prophets?” — the proverb captures the astonishment at unexpected divine action: the one nobody thought was a prophet is prophesying. The same pattern of shocked recognition recurs with Christ: “Who is this? Even the winds and the waves obey him!” (Matthew 8:27); “Where did this man get these things?” (Mark 6:2); “Is not this the carpenter?” (Mark 6:3). Those who knew Jesus from Nazareth were astonished at his authority — the unexpected divine activity in an ordinary person. The question “Is Saul also among the prophets?” anticipates the ultimate question: “Who do you say that I am?” (Matthew 16:15).
12: The anonymous questioner’s response — “And who is their father?” — redirects the origin question from human genealogy to divine origin. A prophet’s “father” in the sense of spiritual source is not a human parent but the Spirit of God. The question anticipates the NT controversy over Jesus’ origin: “Is this not the carpenter’s son?... Where then did this man get all these things?” (Matthew 13:55-56). Christ’s true “father” is not Joseph but God (John 1:14; 1 Samuel 8:19). The proverb “Is Saul also among the prophets?” becomes a standing saying about unexpected divine activity — pointing forward to the ultimate surprise of the Incarnation: God himself among the humans.
13: After prophesying, Saul goes to the high place — the Spirit-anointed king returning to worship. The pattern of Spirit-experience leading to worship is the normative sequence: Pentecost is followed by “they devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer” (Acts 2:42). The Spirit’s coming produces devotion rather than pride. Christ himself after his anointing at baptism goes into the wilderness — a form of going apart for preparation — before beginning public ministry. The anointed one’s movement between Spirit-encounter and the place of meeting God is the rhythm of the messianic life.
14: Saul’s uncle asks where he has been, and Saul gives the surface answer — looking for donkeys — while concealing the deeper reality of his anointing. The concealment of the messianic appointment is a recurring pattern: Christ repeatedly tells people not to speak of his identity until the appointed time (“the messianic secret” in Mark’s Gospel, e.g., Mark 1:44; 1 Samuel 5:43; 1 Samuel 7:36). The hidden king, the concealed anointing, the appointment not yet made public — these are the marks of the period between election and enthronement. Christ was anointed in history but not yet publicly declared until the resurrection (Romans 1:4: “declared to be the Son of God with power... by his resurrection from the dead”).
15: The uncle’s pressing question — “tell me what Samuel said to you” — is the kind of inquiry that seeks the divine word behind the human encounter. The desire to know what the prophet said is the desire for divine disclosure. Every generation’s seeking to understand what Christ “really said” is the same instinct: the encounter that changes everything has a word behind it that others want to access. The disciples’ question “Lord, are you at this time going to restore the kingdom to Israel?” (Acts 1:6) is the same pressing question the uncle asks — what exactly was said about the kingdom?
16: Saul’s silence about the kingship (wədaḇar hamməlûkāh lōʾ-higgîd-lô) is notable. He tells the donkey-news but not the kingdom-news. The theme of partial disclosure — some things reported, the central thing concealed — is the pattern of the period of hidden preparation. Christ kept many things private until the appointed time; the parables conceal as well as reveal (“the secret of the kingdom of God has been given to you. But to those on the outside everything is said in parables”, Mark 4:11). The anointed one’s discretion about his mission is part of the divine economy of revelation.
17: Samuel calls the assembly at Mizpah — the place of covenant before YHWH. Mizpah (“watchtower”) was one of the principal covenant-assembly sites for Israel (Judges 10:17; 1 Samuel 11:11; 1 Samuel 20:1,3; 1 Samuel 7:5-6). The gathering of all Israel before YHWH for the formal installation of the king mirrors the Sinai assembly — the whole people before God for constitutional covenant action. The NT fulfillment is the new covenant assembly: “you have come to Mount Zion... to the church of the firstborn” (Hebrews 12:22-23), the eschatological gathering of all YHWH’s people before the heavenly throne.
18: Samuel’s recitation of the Exodus salvation — “I brought Israel up out of Egypt and rescued you from the power of Egypt and all the kingdoms that were oppressing you” — is the covenant-preamble formula (cf. Exodus 20:2; Deuteronomy 5:6: “I am the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt”). YHWH’s identity is established through his saving acts. Christ’s identity is similarly established through his salvific action — the new Exodus he accomplishes (Luke 9:31 uses exodon for his death; 1 Corinthians 5:7: “Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed”). The God of the Exodus who speaks to Samuel is the same God incarnate in Christ who accomplishes the final Exodus from sin and death.
19: The indictment — “you have rejected your God who saves you from every calamity... and said ‘put a king over us’” — is the theological assessment of the monarchy’s foundation: it began in the rejection of YHWH’s kingship. The NT frames Christ’s rejection similarly: “He came to that which was his own, but his own did not receive him” (John 1:11). The pattern of YHWH being rejected as king by his own people — first in Samuel’s day, finally at the cross (“we have no king but Caesar”, John 19:15) — is the great irony of the covenant: Israel rejects YHWH as king and asks for a human substitute, then rejects the human-divine King when he actually comes. The institution that began in rejection of YHWH ends in the rejection of YHWH incarnate.
20: Benjamin chosen by lot — the smallest tribe. Benjamin being chosen first is itself a reversal of expectation (the largest tribe, Judah, might have been expected to produce the king). The lot-choice of Benjamin, the smallest, continues the pattern of divine election of the unexpected (1 Corinthians 1:27-28: “God chose the weak things of the world... the lowly things... the despised things — and the things that are not — to nullify the things that are”). The Davidic line that will eventually supersede Saul will come from Judah — the lion’s tribe (Genesis 49:10) — but even the Davidic messiah is described as coming from Bethlehem, small among Judah’s clans (Micah 5:2).
21: Saul chosen by lot but nowhere to be found — the elected king in hiding. The hidden king who must be sought out is a shadow of the hiddenness of Christ: born in obscurity, unknown to the powerful, hidden in Egypt, raised in Nazareth (“can anything good come from there?”, John 1:46), unknown to the religious establishment until his public ministry. The chosen king in hiding who must be searched out before he can be installed is the pattern of the Messiah’s concealment — present in the world but not yet publicly manifest.
22: “He has hidden himself among the luggage” (hinnēh hûʾ nistāʾ ʾel-hakkelîm) — the king-elect hidden among ordinary things. The theme of the divine hidden in the ordinary runs through the entire biblical narrative and climaxes in the Incarnation: God hidden in human flesh, the King hidden in a carpenter from Nazareth. The kenosis of Philippians 2:7 (“he made himself nothing, taking the very nature of a servant... he humbled himself”) is the theological articulation of what Saul’s hiding among the luggage anticipates in miniature — though Saul hides from fear, Christ’s condescension was willing and purposeful.
23: When Saul is brought out of hiding, he stands “a head taller than everyone else” — the king is revealed by his height, the external mark the people desired. The contrast with Christ’s revelation could not be sharper: Christ is revealed not by physical stature but by the Spirit’s testimony (John 1:32-34), by his works (John 10:25), and ultimately by his resurrection (Romans 1:4). The people can see Saul’s greatness with their eyes; Christ’s greatness requires the spiritual sight that the Spirit gives (John 3:3; Matthew 16:17: “flesh and blood did not reveal this to you, but my Father in heaven”).
24: “Long live the king!” (yĕḥî hammelek) — the first royal acclamation in Israel. The shout of the people for the anointed king is the prototype of the Messianic cry. “Hosanna!” (“save now!”) at the Triumphal Entry (Matthew 21:9, citing Psalms 118:25-26) is the completion of this acclamation for the true Messiah. The crowd in Jerusalem shouts “Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord” — the royal acclamation that began with Saul finds its object in Jesus. The tragic irony: five days after the Hosanna, the same city shouts “Crucify him!” — the acclamation giving way to rejection, which itself is the paradox that produces salvation.
25: Samuel writes down the rights and duties of the kingship (mišpaṭ hamməlûkāh) and deposits it before YHWH — the constitutional document of the monarchy. The written covenant document deposited before YHWH is the pattern of Mosaic covenant: Exodus 24:7 (the Book of the Covenant read aloud before the people); Deuteronomy 31:26 (the Torah placed beside the ark). The kingdom of Christ is likewise constituted by a covenant word — the new covenant in his blood (Luke 22:20; Hebrews 8:6-13) — written not on stone or scroll but on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3).
26: “Men whose hearts God had moved” (ḥayil ʾăšer-nāgaʿ ʾelōhîm bəlibbām) accompany Saul — those divinely drawn to the new king. The same pattern surrounds Christ: the disciples are drawn to him by the Father (John 6:44: “No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws them”). Those who follow the king are those whose hearts the Spirit has moved — the same divine action that drew Israel’s valiant men to Saul draws sinners to Christ. The formation of a loyal band around the newly installed king anticipates the gathering of the Church around the risen Christ.
27: The worthless men (bənê bəliyyaʿal) who despise Saul and bring him no gift — saying “how is this man going to save us?” (mah-yōšîʿēnû zeh) — are the type of those who rejected Christ: “He came to that which was his own, but his own did not receive him” (John 1:11). The specific question — “how will this man save us?” — is the question of unbelief about the Messiah. The crowd at the cross echoes it: “He saved others... but he can’t save himself!” (Matthew 27:42). Saul’s silent response (“Saul said nothing”) anticipates Christ before his accusers: “he did not open his mouth” (Isaiah 53:7; Matthew 27:14).
